title: Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-region
exclusions: Does NOT detect misconfigured cloud replication within an allowed region; see UC-22.42.2.
evidence: Saved search 'data_localization_breach' runs hourly and writes localization-breach findings to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Detects transfers that violate localization-based lawful-basis constraints expressed via SCCs or national derogations.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of GDPR Art.44 (International transfers — general principle) — Splunk UC-22.38.3: Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-region.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.38.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.28 ICT third-party risk — localization commitments in outsourcing contracts; the UC evidences compliance with contracted hosting regions.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.28 (ICT third-party risk) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.38.3: Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-region.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.38.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.44 (parent assurance='full'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of UK GDPR Art.44 — Splunk UC-22.38.3: Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-region.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.38.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a data object tagged regulated_scope=EU,CH,UK is observed in a cloud storage bucket whose region is outside the allowed_regions list for that scope, the UC fires naming the bucket, region, and classification.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Objects that match the allowed-regions list do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=cloud_storage sourcetype=aws:s3:objectaccess OR sourcetype=azure:blob:access earliest=-1h
| lookup data_classification.csv key=object_key OUTPUT classification regulated_scope
| lookup localization_policy.csv regulated_scope OUTPUT allowed_regions
| where isnotnull(regulated_scope) AND NOT match(bucket_region, allowed_regions)
| table _time bucket bucket_region object_key classification regulated_scope allowed_regions
description: Evaluates data-localization commitments at the object-access layer. A regulated object that appears in the wrong region is a hard policy breach — and the cloud storage audit log is the definitive evidence.
value: Replaces 'we think the bucket is in eu-west-1' with 'we proved every access was in-region for the full audit period'.
implementation: (1) Tag objects with data-classification via the cloud provider's object metadata; (2) maintain localization_policy.csv keyed by regulated_scope; (3) run hourly; (4) escalate breaches via SOAR. Indexes required: `index=cloud_storage`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=aws:s3:objectaccess`, `sourcetype=azure:blob:access`.
visualization: Table of breaches by bucket, bar chart of breaches by scope, single-value '% of accesses in sanctioned region'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for AWS" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `cloud_storage` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `localization_policy.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('localization_policy.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.44`. The control objective is: "Detects violations of GDPR Art.44 (International transfers — general principle) — Splunk UC-22.38.3: Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-region." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876), landing events in `cloud_storage` under `aws:s3:objectaccess`, `azure:blob:access`. CloudTrail management events average 5000-50000 API calls/account/hour for active workloads (~5-50 GB/account/month). Data events (S3, Lambda) can multiply this 10-100x.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > CloudTrail (SQS-based ingest is strongly preferred over S3 polling). A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[aws_cloudtrail://prod_account]
aws_account = production
aws_region = us-east-1
sqs_queue = splunk-ct-queue
index = aws
sourcetype = aws:cloudtrail
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `eventName, eventSource, userIdentity.arn, sourceIPAddress, requestParameters, responseElements, errorCode, userAgent`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=cloud_storage earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Tag objects with data-classification via the cloud provider's object metadata. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** maintain localization_policy.csv keyed by regulated_scope. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** run hourly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** escalate breaches via SOAR. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-region," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `localization_policy.csv` — the policy register codifying the approved configuration baseline. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`allowed_regions`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `regulated_scope`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `localization_policy.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval regulated_scope = lower(regulated_scope)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last hour.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.38.3: Data localization enforcement — regulated-data must-stay-in-]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -1h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.38.3,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=cloud_storage sourcetype=aws:s3:objectaccess earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `allowed_regions`, `bucket`, `bucket_region`, `classification`, `object_key`, `regulated_scope`. Run:

```spl
index=cloud_storage sourcetype=aws:s3:objectaccess earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, allowed_regions, bucket, bucket_region, classification, object_key, regulated_scope
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `localization_policy.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup localization_policy.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.38.3: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Breaches By Bucket (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Breaches By Scope (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Accesses In Sanctioned Region' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see cross-border data flows against your rules so transfers stay justified and documented.
